coredata.dc:title: Halogen-doped CQDs as a modulation of fractional function sensing in ZIF composites

coredata.dc:description: This study draws inspiration from the partition-function sensing mechanism of plant cells for purifying harmful gas molecules. The size of the pore size of ZIF-8 sieving gas molecules was modulated by halogen-doped carbon quantum dots (CQDs) during the formation of ZIF-8. Notably, the difference in electronegativity of different halogens makes the sieving pore size of ZIF-8 tunable in the nanometer range, which greatly enhances the selectivity of the composite. In addition, the halogen-doped carbon quantum dots modulate the structure, band gap, and impedance of the composites, enabling the detection of specific gases on the order of ppb. Using X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy, simulation data and density-functional theory, we find that halogen-doped CQDs can fine-tune the pore size of ZIF-8 by influencing the chemical bond lengths by affecting the positions of atoms on the imidazole ring. Plants in nature are similarly characterized in that different components act as sensors for characteristic gas components and synergistically promote the adsorption and purification processes of gas molecules. This study provides an innovative means of modifying the structure and properties of ZIF materials and introduces the concept of plant-like gas multi-sensing in the field of gas sensing research.

head.ce:title: Halogen-doped CQDs as a modulation of fractional function sensing in ZIF composites

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: This study draws inspiration from the partition-function sensing mechanism of plant cells for purifying harmful gas molecules. The size of the pore size of ZIF-8 sieving gas molecules was modulated by halogen-doped carbon quantum dots (CQDs) during the formation of ZIF-8. Notably, the difference in electronegativity of different halogens makes the sieving pore size of ZIF-8 tunable in the nanometer range, which greatly enhances the selectivity of the composite. In addition, the halogen-doped carbon quantum dots modulate the structure, band gap, and impedance of the composites, enabling the detection of specific gases on the order of ppb. Using X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy, simulation data and density-functional theory, we find that halogen-doped CQDs can fine-tune the pore size of ZIF-8 by influencing the chemical bond lengths by affecting the positions of atoms on the imidazole ring. Plants in nature are similarly characterized in that different components act as sensors for characteristic gas components and synergistically promote the adsorption and purification processes of gas molecules. This study provides an innovative means of modifying the structure and properties of ZIF materials and introduces the concept of plant-like gas multi-sensing in the field of gas sensing research.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: Certain plants (such as greens and hanging orchids) have the ability to absorb and detoxify harmful gases such as formaldehyde due to their unique physiological and biochemical properties. Their broad leaf surfaces engage in extensive gas exchange with their surroundings, and leaf pores trap carbon dioxide and harmful formaldehyde. In addition, enzymes such as formaldehyde dehydrogenase (FDH) are present in green plants, which react against gases such as formaldehyde [1]. Also, pigments or chlorophylls, which are unique to green plants, convert carbon dioxide into organic compounds and oxygen. This discovery inspired us to mimic the multi-sensory system of plants. In this system, gas-sensing properties such as material selectivity and sensitivity are contributed by different units, but the independent units synergize to contribute to the overall performance. The idea of multiple sensing of gases in plants will give researchers a deeper understanding of the source expression of gas-sensitive properties [2].

In the complex harmony of materials, zinc oxide (ZnO) stands out as a usual n-type semiconductor, embellished with a wide bandgap (3.2–3.4 eV) and a significant exciton binding energy (60 meV) [3]. ZnO has been extensively used for detecting toxic and flammable gases and volatile organic compounds (VOCs) [4–7]. Yet, it faces challenges such as elevated operating temperatures, low responsiveness, poor selectivity, and susceptibility to humidity [8–10]. ZIF-8 is a member of the imidazoline zeolite framework (ZIF) family and consists of tetrahedral Zn2+ metal ions bridged with imidazolium esters (Im) in a zeolite-like topology [11,12]. Employing a metal oxide semiconductor (MOS) as a self-sacrificing template for metal ion synthesis facilitated the construction of an MOS@ZIF composite structure [13–15]. These frameworks exhibited enduring porosity coupled with high thermal and chemical stability [16]. Insightful studies have highlighted the pivotal role of hexanuclear rings within ZIF-8 in orchestrating the ingress of guest molecules through the pores and the adsorption of gaseous molecules [17–19]. ZIF-8′s molecular sieving prowess have been leveraged for the separation of meta-xylene isomers and bio-alcohols [20,21]. Tian et al. deftly harnessed ZIF-8′s molecular sieving characteristic to fashion a ZnO@ZIF-8 sensor, elevating its formaldehyde discernment [22]. Similarly, Drobek orchestrated enhanced selectivity for hydrogen using this strategy [23]. The hydrophobicity of ZIF-8 effectively bolsters the humidity resilience of ZnO@ZIF-8 gas sensors [22,24]. However, most ZIF materials have poor electrical conductivity in atmospheric environments, and the large surface area and porous nature of ZIF are not fully utilized to detect changes in electrical signals during gas molecule detection, affecting the gas-sensing performance of ZIF-based composites [25,26]. Thus, in the future application of ZIF in the field of gas sensing, there are two issues that need to be broken through: (1) how to improve the detection ability of ZIF composites for gases with ppb concentration at room temperature; (2) how to flexibly utilize the adsorption and screening molecular properties of ZIF materials without being limited by the fixed pore size of each ZIF material for screening gases. Carbon quantum dots (CQDs), which are diminutive entities with dimensions of less than 10 nm, comprise carbonaceous frameworks and surface functional groups [27,28]. These CQDs exhibited rapid photoinduced electron transfer capabilities and excellent electronic conductivity in their graphitic cores [29,30]. Researchers have found applications for CQDs in the field of gas sensitivity in their explorations. In 2019, Wang et al. presented CQD-embedded mesoporous silica for swift fluorescence detection of acidic gases [31]. In the same year, Liu et al. presented 3D ordered mesoporous ZnO adorned with graphene quantum dots, which served as a responsive material for the detection of acetone [32]. In 2020, Yu et al. doped ZnO with CQDs via a milling method using the abundant active functional groups of the CQDs to detect NO [33]. Several other studies have shown that the abundant functional groups on the surface of CQDs can interact with metal ions to form a three-dimensional network structure, thus effectively inhibiting the aggregation and growth of metal nanoparticles [34,35]. This inspired us whether CQDs can be utilized to modify ZIF-8 during the self-templated formation of ZIF-8 on the ZnO surface, and to modulate the structure of ZIF-8 by influencing its growth process through CQDs, which affects its specific surface area and sieve pore size. It is also desired that the CQDs act as a bridge between ZnO and ZIF-8 to facilitate charge transfer and optimize the electrical properties of the material in order to promote the ability of the composite to detect low concentration gases at room temperature [36–38]. Halogens, renowned for their high specificity and directionality, have been employed as potent structural modulators in crystal engineering and other fields [39,40]. Due to the weak gradient differences in electronegativity of the same main group of halogens, it is expected that the halogens are combined with carbon quantum dots to more finely tune the size of the ZIF-8 sieve aperture, which is thus comparable to the diameter of the gas molecule. Within this structure, CQDs act as carriers for halogens, preventing damage to the ZIF-8 structure by acidic and alkaline gases released from the precursors of halogens during experiments, and they also prevent ionic aggregation [41,42].

In this paper, the physicochemical properties of ZIF-8 were synergistically modulated by CQDs and halogens (F, Cl and Br) on the basis of ZnO composite ZIF-8 core–shell nanorods. In terms of physical structure, the sieve pore size of ZIF-8 can be finely tunable at the nanometer level due to the difference of doped halogens, and the modified composite has a more favorable adsorption structure, and experiments and calculations demonstrate that the selective adsorption capacity of the ZIF material is enhanced. In addition, the electrical properties of the material, such as conductivity, were improved, which significantly enhanced the low concentration (ppb) detection ability of the material.

Zinc nitrate hexahydrate (Zn(NO3)2·6H2O, 99 %), sodium hydroxide (NaOH, 96 %), 2-methylimidazole (2-Melm), L-Ascorbic acid (C6H8O6), ammonium fluoride (NH4F), ammonium bromide (NH4Br), ammonium chloride (NH4Cl), lead nitrate (Pb(NO3)2), chloroplatinic acid (H₂PtCl₆), a saturated aqueous solution of K2SO4 was added to the flask to obtain 96 % RH, N, N-dimethylformamide (C3H7NO, 99.5 %), hydrogen peroxide (H2O2, 30 %), ethylene glycol (CH₂OH, 98 %), N-methyl pyrrolidone (C5H9NO, 97 %), ammonia (NH3·H2O 25 %), acetone (C3H6O, 99.5 %), xylene (C8H10, 98.5 %), toluene (C7H8, 99.5 %), formaldehyde (CH2O, 36 %), ethylenediamine (C2H8N2, 99 %), methanol (CH4O, 99.8 %), ethanol (C2H6O, 95 %) were purchased from commercial suppliers (Sinopharm Chemical Regent Co., Ltd.). All the chemicals were used in the as-received state without additional purification. Deionized water was used in all the experiments.

Zinc oxide nanorods (ZnO NBs): We made slight adjustments to the experimental synthesis route described in [43]. Initially, Zn(NO3)2·6H2O (2.26 g) and NaOH (2.43 g) were dissolved in deionized water (40 mL) and stirred for 30 min. Subsequently, the stirred white solution was transferred to a reaction vessel and heated at 140 °C for 12 h. The reaction mixture was allowed to cool to room temperature. The solution was centrifuged at 1000 rpm and the collected white precipitate was washed three times with deionized water and ethanol. The obtained precipitate was dried at 60 °C for 8 h. The dried white solid was ground into a fine powder, heated to 500 °C, and maintained for 2 h, ultimately yielding the ZnO nanorods.

ZnO@ZIF-8 nanorods (ZZ): ZIF-8 was grown on the surface of ZnO using a chemical bath method. ZnO nanorods (0.0814 g) and 2-methylimidazole (0.648 g) were added to a mixed solution of DMF/DI and DI (80 mL, v/v ratio of 3:1). The resulting solution was stirred for 30 min and sonicated for 5 min. Subsequently, the reaction mixture was heated at 70 °C for 24 h. The white precipitate was collected by centrifugation at 1000 rpm, washed three times with DMF and ethanol, and finally dried at 60 °C for 6 h. This process resulted in the formation of ZnO@ZIF-8 core–shell nanorods.

Carbon quantum dots (CQDs): CQDs were synthesized using a bottom-up methodology, as shown in Fig. 2(a). A solution of l-ascorbic acid (2 g) and EG/DI mixed solvent (50 mL, v/v = 2:3) was stirred for 30 min, followed by heating at 160 °C for 1 h. Simultaneously, CQDs dispersions doped with halogen elements (F, Cl, and Br) were generated by introducing NH4F, NH4Cl, or NH4Br (0.857 g) during stirring (Fig. S7).

ZnO/(F/Cl/Br-)CQDs@ZIF-8 (ZZC, ZZF, ZZCl, and ZZBr): In the hydrothermal reaction where ZIF-8 is etched on the surface of ZnO, we introduced carbon quantum dots (40 µL) dispersion to form the ZnO/CQDs@ZIF-8 composite material (ZZC), as shown in Fig. 1 . Furthermore, composite materials were prepared using F-, Cl-, and Br-doped carbon quantum dots (ZZF, ZZCl, and ZZBr, respectively). To assess the optimal content of carbon quantum dots and their impact on the testing performance, we also prepared ZnO/Cl-CQDs@ZIF-8 (ZZCl-20, ZZCl-60) for comparison.

The thoroughly ground material was mixed with a small quantity of deionized water and uniformly coated onto a fork-finger silver electrode located on the ceramic substrate using a brush (Fig. S1(a)). Subsequently, it was allowed to air dry naturally at room temperature (∼22 ℃) for 24 h. Gas sensitivity experiments were conducted at room temperature using an AES-4SD flexible electronic integrated test platform (Fig. S1(b)). Specific gas concentrations were prepared by thermal evaporation. The response was calculated using the formula ΔI/Iair = (Igas-Iair)/Iair , where Iair and Igas represent the current in air and the target gas, respectively. The response time indicates the duration required to attain 90 % of the stabilized response value, whereas the recovery time is defined as the time taken to return to within 10 % of the initial response value.

XRD analysis was conducted using a Bruker D8 Advance instrument from Germany, employing Cu Kα1 (λ = 0.154 nm) radiation to determine the crystalline phase of the samples. Scanning electron microscopy (SEM) observations, providing insights into the surface micromorphology and elemental distribution, were performed using a Oberkochen LEO1430VP instrument (Germany). TEM analysis, aimed at examining the composite structures and carbon quantum dot loading, was performed using a Tecnai G2 F30 instrument (USA). Leveraging AC-TEM (FEI Titan Cubed Themis G2 300, Netherlands), we performed an in-depth investigation of the spatial distribution of the photodeposited particles. UV–Vis testing of light absorption at different wavelengths was performed using a Hitachi U-3010 instrument (Japan) calibrated with Ba2SO4. To analyze the surface chemical states, XPS was employed with a Thermo ESCALAB 250XI instrument (USA), whereas FTIR analysis utilized equipment from Bruker-V D8 Advance (Germany). The BET surface area and pore structure were determined using a nitrogen adsorption analyzer (Micromeritics ASAP 2460, Norcross GA, USA). The electrical properties of the materials were investigated using an electrochemical workstation (CHI760E, Shanghai CH instruments, China).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Photocatalytic deposition of Pt and PbO2
ce:section.ce:para: The samples were subjected to deposition of Pt and PbO2 using H2PtCl6 and Pb(NO3)2 as the respective precursors. In this process, the sample (50 mg) and 2 wt% of the precursor were added to a methanol (10 mL) solution with continuous stirring. The suspensions were irradiated with a 300 W Xe lamp (AM 1.5) until the liquids volatilized. This resulted in the formation of ZZCl-Pt and ZZCl-PbO2 samples.

The initial computation was conducted using the sorption module in Materials Studio. The computational model utilizes the atomic position data obtained after Rietveld refinement. In terms of the parameter configurations, the pressure and temperature were held constant using the Metropolis method. The fugacity of the adsorbed molecules was set to 100.0 KPa, with equilibration and production steps of 10-6 and 10-7, respectively. The force field adopted was COMPASS III, and the charge was designated as the force field assigned. The summation method was atom-based, and the quality was customized.

For first-principles calculations, we utilized the Vienna ab initio simulation package (VASP) and the projector-augmented wave (PAW) method. The Perdew–Burke–Ernzerhof (PBE) generalized gradient approximation was employed in this process. The primitive cell structure of ZIF-8 was optimized, and a ZIF-8 cluster model was adopted to elucidate the surface state of ZIF-8. In the structural optimization process, we employed a plane-wave cutoff energy of 500 eV, along with Gaussian smearing of 0.05 eV for integration over the Brillouin zone. Allowing all atoms to relax within the cell, we ensured that total energy and Hellman-Feynman force tolerance were below 1.0 × 10-8 eV/atom and 0.02 eV/Å, respectively. Spin polarization was considered throughout the calculations. Owing to the substantial size of the system, Brillouin zone sampling was confined to the gamma points. A lattice of 20 Å in size was constructed to calculate the independent molecules.

The crystalline nature of the F/Cl/Br-CQDs is evident from the sharp diffraction peaks, as illustrated in Fig. 2 (b). The addition of halogen ions (F- Cl-, and Br-) during CQDs generation significantly enhanced crystallinity [44–46]. Notably, in comparison to the Cl/Br-CQDs, the peak of the F-CQDs exhibited a lower angle shift, indicating an expansion of its unit cell. The high electronegativity of fluorine may induce alterations in the local charge distribution. This charge effect probably interacts with the electronic cloud of the lattice, thereby causing changes in the lattice parameters. As shown in Fig. 2(c), the infrared spectra reveal analogous chemical bonds and functional groups across all the CQDs materials. The prominent peaks between 3300–3700 cm−1 corresponded to the hydroxyl (–OH) and amino (–NH2) groups’ stretching vibrations. These active –OH groups facilitate reductive activity and transform adsorbed oxygen into negative-valence ions [47]. The symmetric stretching vibrations of –CH3 and –CH2 are represented by the peaks at 2954 cm−1 and 2885 cm−1, respectively. The stretching of C = C/C = O was denoted by the band at 1647 cm−1. The presence of the C-O bond contributed to the peak at 1450 cm−1, while the symmetric and asymmetric stretching vibrations of C-O were observed at 1087 cm−1 and 1200 cm−1, respectively [48]. The transmission electron microscopy (TEM) image (Fig. 2(d)) displays clearly defined black particles identified as CQDs with an average size of 3 nm. The lattice spacing of the Cl-CQDs was measured at 0.24 nm [49–51].

ce:section.ce:section-title: Morphological structure of halogen-doped carbon dot composites
ce:section.ce:para: The XRD plot in Fig. 3 (a) shows that the diffraction peaks of all the materials conform to the standard PDF card for ZnO (PDF#99–0111), with sharp peaks indicating the formation of well-crystallized ZnO materials. Notably, the peaks concentrated in the 5°–30° range for the composite materials aligned with the calculated simulation diffraction peaks of ZIF-8 with a cubic space group (I—43 m) (CCDC-864312). This indicated the successful incorporation of ZnO into ZIF-8. ZZC, ZZF, ZZCl, and ZZBr exhibit peaks at the same diffraction angles. The CQDs, acting as carriers doped with halogen elements, protect the unique pore structure of ZIF-8 from destruction and ensure the uniform doping of halogen elements into the material. Owing to the relatively low doping amount of CQDs in the composite materials, the corresponding peaks for F-, Cl-, and Br-CQDs were not detected in the XRD analysis. A comparative analysis of the relative height changes in the diffraction peaks of ZZC, ZZF, ZZCl, and ZZBr revealed that the F/Cl/Br-CQDs facilitated the growth of the composite materials along the (100), (002), and (101) crystal facets of ZnO, corresponding to 31.9°, 34.5°, and 36.4°, respectively. Similarly, a noticeable promoting effect was observed along the (011) and (112) crystal facets of ZIF-8, with a clear enhancement in the crystallinity with increasing atomic numbers of F, Cl, and Br.

This demonstrates that the introduction of halogen-doped carbon quantum dots in the design and construction of three-dimensional core–shell ZnO@ZIF-8 composite materials enables rational adjustment of the directional growth of the nanomaterial. Fig. 3(b) shows the Fourier-transform infrared (FTIR) spectra of all materials. Displaying characteristic attributes, the FT-IR spectrum of ZIF-8 reveals absorption peaks at 3135 cm−1 and 2929 cm−1. These peaks correspond to the asymmetric stretching vibrations of the aromatic C-H in the imidazole ring and the stretching vibrations of the aliphatic C-H in the methyl group, respectively [52]. In the spectral landscape, the peaks in the ranges of 500–1350 cm−1 and 1350–1500 cm−1 align with the planar bending and stretching of the imidazole ring. Simultaneously, the peak at 1583 cm−1 was assigned to the stretching vibration of C = N. Furthermore, at 1146 cm−1 and 995 cm−1, we encountered resonances linked to the stretching and bending vibrations of aromatic C-N, respectively [53]. At 755 cm−1 and 691 cm−1, absorption bands arise, signifying Zn-O and Zn-N interactions, respectively [54]. The XRD patterns were analyzed using the GSAS software with Rietveld refinement employing the Lorentzian peak profile function. The experimental diffraction data aligned well with the calculated curves (Figs. S2(a-f)). The crystallographic information of the materials was refined and is presented in Table 1 . A comparison of the mass fractions of the two phases in the composite reveals that the introduction of carbon quantum dots reduces the mass fraction of ZIF-8. The thickness of the ZIF-8 shell is crucial for ion transfer in the composite, affecting changes in the electrochemical and gas-sensitive performance [55,56]. However, the introduction of F-CQDs increases the thickness of the ZIF-8 shell, potentially reducing the rate of charge-carrier transfer in the material. Compared to ZZC, ZZCl and ZZBr further decreased the mass fraction of ZIF-8.

SEM images in Fig. 4 (a, b) reveal the synthesis of ZnO nanorods, with lengths ranging from to 2–8 μm and diameters around 0.5 μm. Fig. 4(c) illustrates the morphology of the ZZ through transmission mapping, displaying a rod-shaped core–shell structure. The ZIF-8 shell, formed by etching 2-methylimidazole on the ZnO surface, had an approximate thickness of 0.5 μm. Fig. 4(d, e), (g, h), (j, k), and (m, n) present the SEM images of ZZC, ZZF, ZZCl, and ZZBr, respectively. All images show distinct core–shell structures with uniform halogen doping, as indicated by elemental mapping. A comparative analysis of the shell thickness indicated a significant reduction in ZZC compared to ZZ, suggesting that the CQDs introduced during ZIF-8 formation systematically controlled the ZIF-8 shell thickness. Carbon quantum dots doped with halogen elements Cl and Br maintained this feature, as shown in Fig. 4(k and n). However, the “anomalous” F-CQDs lead to an increased thickness of the ZIF-8 shell, consistent with data on phase mass ratios in Table 1. The SEM plot shows that the CQDs as carriers can build complete three-dimensional structures [57]. The EDS elemental spectrum of Fig. S3 shows the elements in ZZCl and their relative contents.

To evaluate their enduring porosities, the composite samples' N2 adsorption isotherms at 298 K are shown in Fig. 5
. The ZnO sample exhibited no distinct adsorption peaks across the entire P/P0 range, indicating weak interaction forces with nitrogen and almost negligible porosity within the material. This structural characteristic is unfavorable for gas adsorption response and efficient gas desorption. As the relative pressure (P/P0) approached 1, both ZZCl and ZZ showed a gradual increase in the adsorption isotherms, displaying the characteristics of reversible type-I isotherms. The plot depicting the distribution of pore sizes indicated a swift surge in the number of micropores following the incorporation of the ZIF-8 composite material. This suggests that the external surface area of the samples was notably smaller than the internal surface area of the pores, elucidating the heightened gas sensitivity response observed in the ZZ materials.

Figs. 6 (a and b) show the optical absorption characteristics of all the materials using UV–visible spectroscopy. Pure-phase ZnO exhibits poor light absorption; however, upon encapsulation in ZIF-8, the heterogeneous structure induces a noticeable redshift in ZZ’s absorption edge of ZZ, accompanied by a reduced bandgap. Loading CQDs resulted in a slightly elevated bandgap compared to that of ZZ owing to the large bandgap of CQDs (∼4.27 eV), which broadened the bandgap of the composite material. The incorporation of halogen elements into CQDs had a minimal impact on the light absorption and bandgap of the composite material. However, notably, among F, Cl, and Br elements, F diminishes the light absorption intensity and increases the bandgap more than Cl and Br, hindering the flow of charge carriers within the materials.

To thoroughly explore the influence of halogen-doped CQDs on the mobility of the surface charge carriers, electrochemical impedance spectroscopy (EIS) measurements were performed on the samples. The frequency tested was 0.01 Hz and the value of Rct derived from the analogue circuit is shown in Table S1. The Nyquist plots in Fig. 7 (a and b) exhibit unclosed semicircles, which are possibly influenced by material and electrolyte interactions. A smaller semicircle radius indicates a reduction in charge transfer resistance. As shown in Fig. 7(a), the arc radius of ZZCl-40 was slightly smaller than that of ZZCl-20, indicating an enhanced charge-carrier transport potential. The ZZCl-60 curve indicates that the excess carbon quantum dot material hinders charge-carrier transport. The arc radius of Z is the largest and hence has the highest impedance, which implies that the charge transfer capability is the weakest. However, after encapsulating ZnO with ZIF-8, ZZ’s radius of ZZ significantly decreased, possibly because the heterojunction effect accelerated the surface charge migration [59]. Introducing CQDs into ZZ significantly reduced the impedance, indicating that CQDs can expedite the migration rate of charge carriers. In contrast, F and Br doping slightly decreased electron migration on the surface of the material, whereas Cl doping reduced the impedance of the composite, achieving the lowest charge transfer resistance and providing an optimal physical environment for charge carrier transfer. Electronic interactions between CQDs and semiconductor materials can be enhanced by heteroatom doping/modification [58]. Compared with the physical milling method, CQDs doping CQDs during the formation of ZIF-8 can better utilize the excellent properties of CQDs [33]. In Fig. 7(c), the Nyquist plot depicting the Warburg diffusion is presented, aligned with the equivalent circuit diagrams shown in Fig. 7(a and b). where RS signifies the solution resistance determined by the electrolyte, Rct represents the charge transfer resistance regulated by the electrochemical reaction kinetics, CPE denotes the double-layer capacitance, and W represents the Warburg impedance contingent on material transfer [60].

XPS was employed to determine the elemental composition and chemical states of all the materials. The C 1s spectrum of Z in Fig. 8 (a) was fitted with a main peak at 284.8 eV, along with weaker peaks at 286.94 eV and 289.25 eV. The main peak at 284.8 eV is attributed to sp2 hybridized carbon, 286.94 eV, carbon hydroxyl (C-OH), and 289.25 eV, to carbonyl carbon, which are closely related to (HO-C = O) [61]. The O 1 s spectrum in Fig. 8(c) revealed fitted peaks at 530.11 eV, 531.78 eV, and 533.18 eV, corresponding to lattice oxygen in ZnO (Zn-O), oxygen in hydroxyl groups at Zn sites (O-H), and adsorbed oxygen species (O2–, O-, O2 –) [62–65]. The Zn 2p spectrum in Fig. 8(d) was fitted with two peaks at 1021.74 eV and 1044.72 eV, which were assigned to Zn 2p3/2 and Zn 2p1/2, respectively [62]. The C 1 s spectrum of ZZ in Fig. 8(e) exhibited two fitted peaks at 284.8 eV and 285.9 eV, corresponding to sp2 hybridized C atoms in the imidazole ring and sp2 C bonds (N = C-N) in ZIF-8 [66]. The C 1 s spectra of many studies on ZnO@ZIF-8 composite materials were fitted similarly to the C 1 s spectrum of ZnO mentioned earlier, displaying three peaks (sp2 hybridized carbon, C-OH, and HO-C = O) [62]. However, after the ZnO rods were coated with ZIF-8, a trend was observed in the composite materials in which the peak associated with carbonyl groups (∼288.6 eV) in the C 1 s spectrum was missing, and the trends in all C 1 s curves of materials with ZIF-8 were consistent. We attribute this to the influence of the ZIF-8 shell; as revealed by SEM, the thickness of the ZIF-8 shell in ZZ is about 0.2 μm, while the analysis depth of the X-ray used for XPS detection is around 1.5 nm (≪ 0.2 μm), and the photons only probe the interior of ZIF-8. It is well known that ZIF-8 molecules do not contain oxygen atoms; hence, the disappearance of the peak associated with C = O bonds indicates that the encapsulation of ZIF-8 on the ZnO surface is in the form of a homogeneous coating. This aligns with the trend of the XPS peaks in the material after doping with carbon dots and the refined data in Table 1. In Fig. 8(f), a characteristic peak of N 1 s appears after ZnO is coated with ZIF-8, fitted at 398.92 eV and 400.31 eV, corresponding to C-N bonds in the pyridine N and pyrrole N (Zn-N bonds) in ZIF-8 [66–68]. The O 1 s of ZZ was fitted with peaks at 531.46 eV attributed to hydroxyl oxygen (O-H) and 533.38 eV due to adsorbed oxygen (O2–, O-, O2 –) [63,69,70]. The disappearance of the peak corresponding to lattice oxygen (Zn-O) was attributed to the ZIF-8 shell. In Fig. 8(h), the Zn 2p spectrum was fitted with peaks at 1022.17 eV (Zn 2p3/2) and 1045.18 eV (Zn 2p1/2). We found that after encapsulation with ZIF-8, the Zn 2p peak shifted to a higher binding energy, the Zn-O bond broke, and the Zn ion coordinated with the highly electronegative 2-MelM and Zn-N bonds. When chemical bonds are formed, energy is released. According to Table 3 , the average bond length of Zn-O bonds in ZIF-8 is 1.980 Å, while the average bond length of Zn-N bonds in ZZ is 1.664 Å. The shorter the bond, the more stable the structure and the higher the energy. In this case, it is inferred that the energy released by the Zn-N bond formation causes a spectral shift.

In the C 1 s spectrum of ZZC, the peak representing the ZIF-8 heterocycle (N = C-N) exhibited a lower binding energy shift of 0.2 eV. Analyzing the bond lengths presented in Table 3 for ZZ and ZZC, this shift may be attributed to the transformation of C1-N1 (1.965 Å), C2-N1 (1.365 Å), and C3-N1 (2.077 Å) in ZZ to C2-N1 (1.509 Å) in ZZC, where the energy required to break the C = N double bond is greater than that for C-N single bond formation and aggregation. In ZZC’s N 1 s spectrum, the binding energy of the Zn-N peak decreases by 0.3 eV, aligning with the reduction in the mass fraction of ZIF-8 in the composite, as noted in Table 1. The shift of 0.11 eV in the binding energy of C-N = C signifies the transition from C = N to C-N, consistent with the observed changes in bond lengths. The O 1 s spectrum of ZZC at 531.35 eV was attributed to oxygen defects, specifically interstitial oxygen atoms. The peak at 532.79 eV, representing chemisorbed oxygen, was significantly larger in ZZC than in ZZ, possibly indicating the strategic influence of the doped CQDs on the growth of ZIF-8. The superior structure of ZIF-8 allowed for substantial adsorption of negative oxygen ions during gas reactions, corroborating the SBET , EIS, and Table 1 results. The Zn 2p fitting peak of ZZC closely resembles that of ZZ.

We consolidated the peak shift sizes corresponding to several bonds, as shown in Table S2. Initially, we observed that the incorporation of F, Cl, and Br led to an elevation in the binding energy of Zn-N bonds in the composite materials. Specifically, the Zn-N bond energies in ZZF, ZZCl, and ZZBr increased by 0.62 eV, 0.58 eV, and 0.49 eV, respectively. This increasing trend in the bond energy aligns closely with the corresponding reduction in the Zn1-N1 bond length, as indicated in Table 3. Compared to ZZC, ZZF, ZZCl, and ZZBr exhibited shorter bond lengths of 0.542, 0.474, and 0.272 Å, respectively. Although one might expect a substantial increase in the mass fraction of ZIF-8 in these three materials, closer examination of Table 1 reveals that only ZZF showed a slight increase in the mass fraction of ZIF-8, whereas ZZCl and ZZBr exhibited a significant decrease. The small radius and large electronegativity of the F atom make it easy for metal ions to bond, allowing ZIF-8 to aggregate. The length of the Zn-N bond can potentially alter the diameter of the hexagonal channels in ZIF-8, thereby influencing the gas transport process for molecules of varying sizes. In Fig. 8, the oxygen adsorption peaks for ZZF, ZZCl, and ZZBr gradually increased, which is consistent with the decreasing trend in the Oabs binding energy outlined in Table 3. This suggests that F, Cl, and Br promoted the loss of electrons from the composite material, transforming the electrons adsorbed by oxygen into oxygen anions that reacted with the gases. This phenomenon was identified as the direct cause of the enhanced gas sensitivity of the material induced by F, Cl, and Br [71]. The peaks around 531 eV in the O 1 s spectra of ZZF, ZZCl, and ZZBr, which are related to the oxygen vacancies (Vo) associated with O2 – ions, are attributed to interstitial defects [72]. Notably, the decrease in the binding energy at the Vo for ZZCl and ZZBr indicates the transformation of oxygen molecules into O2 – through electron gain.

We further investigated the photoelectron spectra of F 1 s, Cl 2p, and Br 3d for ZZF, ZZCl, and ZZBr. The F 1 s spectrum in Fig. 9 (a) was fitted with peaks at 685.97 eV (C-F) and 683.21 eV (Zn-F) [73–75]. The C-F bond structure forms a covalent bond through a semi-ionic bond, where the higher electronegativity of fluorine compared to that of carbon enhances the polarity/dipole moment, thereby increasing the rate of electron/ion transfer [76,77]. In Fig. 9(b), the Cl 2p peaks at 199.87 eV and 198.17 eV represent the Cl 2p1/2 and Cl 2p2/3 peaks, respectively [78,79]. The possible peak splitting of elemental Cl in ZZCl is shown in Fig. S4 [80]. Fig. 9(c) displays the Br 3d peaks at 69.2 eV and 68.1 eV for Br 3d3/2 and Br 3d5/2, respectively, with the peak at 68.4 eV indicating the presence of Br−, which can form bonds with Zn elements [81,82]. During the synthesis of ZIF-8, its surface was modified with 2-methylimidazole, a proton-stripping ligand. The highly electronegative 2-methylimidazole coordinates with the metal ions, resulting in a positive charge on the ZIF-8 [83]. Therefore, Anions such as F−, Cl−, and Br− can interact with ZIF-8 [38].

ZZCl’s performance of ZZCl as a sensor for various gases was examined at room temperature (∼22 ℃), as illustrated in Fig. 10 (a). Notably, ZZCl exhibited high sensitivity to 500 ppm NH3, demonstrating robust repeatability over three gas cycles, with an average response time of 7 s and a recovery time of 3 s. This underscores ZZCl’s ability for swift NH3 response and desorption. Further investigation of ZZCl’s response of ZZCl to different gases revealed interesting findings. The response current to formaldehyde aligns with the literature on ZnO/ZIF-8 sensors; however, the introduction of Cl-CQDs during the zeolitic imidazole framework formation significantly reduces the response of the material to formaldehyde, while enhancing its responsiveness to ammonia. A comparative analysis of ZZCl’s current variations in ZZCl for other gases (Fig. 10(b–k)) revealed markedly lower responses to CH2O, H2O2, C2H6O, CH4O, C5H9NO, C3H6O, C8H10, C2H8N2, C7H8, and O3 at 500 ppm compared to NH3, highlighting ZZCl’s pronounced selectivity of ZZCl for NH3. The data in Fig. 10(l) show that the response of ZZCl at 96 % relative humidity (RH) is very low compared with the response of the target gas, which highlights the strong resistance of ZZCl to moisture interference. From Fig. 3(b), it can be found that after ZIF-8 composite, the stretching vibration peaks of –CH3 appeared in the infrared spectra, in which the hydrophobicity of the composite is mainly due to the functional groups in ZIF-8 are not favourable to the solid to absorb water, in addition, the literature suggests that Cl doping to form the functional groups around the CQDs also promotes the hydrophobicity of the material [84]. Fig. 11 (a–c) presents a comparative analysis of ZZF, ZZCl, and ZZBr in terms of their response values, response times, and recovery times to 500 ppm of different gases. ZZBr’s response current curves of ZZBr for CH2O and NH3 are shown in Fig. S5, where it exhibits a notably high response current change to formaldehyde, with an average response time of 23 s and an average recovery time of 4 s. However, ZZBr’s response of ZZBr to NH3 was considerably lower than that to CH2O. As illustrated in Fig. 11(a), ZZBr demonstrates relatively low responses to other gases, indicating its potential as a gas sensor with a specific selectivity for formaldehyde. Furthermore, ZZF exhibited intriguing specificity in response to methanol and ethanol, with an average response time of 13 s and an average recovery time of 3 s. Thus, ZZF holds promise as a gas sensor with specific responsiveness to alcohols (methanol and ethanol).

To assess the theoretical detection limits (LOD) and stability of ZZCl and ZZBr for NH3 and CH2O, respectively, we tested their response currents to different gas concentrations, as depicted in Fig. 12 (a and d).

Notably, ZZCl exhibited a response of 6 value to 2 ppm NH3, whereas ZZBr showed a response of 4 value to 6 ppm CH2O. The calculation of the limit of detection (LOD) theoretically follows the formula LOD = 3SD/m, where m represents the slope of the linear segment of the calibration curve and SD is the standard deviation of noise observed in the response curve [93]. As shown in Fig. 12(b and e), ZZCl, and ZZBr demonstrated a linear relationship with the concentration at low levels, with standard deviations SD of 0.006428 and 0.006157, respectively. The calculated LOD for ZZCl regarding NH3 and ZZBr regarding CH2O were found to be 6.8 ppb and 9.2 ppb, highlighting the high sensitivity of ZZCl and ZZBr to NH3 and CH2O, respectively. The halogen-doped quantum dot interface with a unique quantum effect and halogen-carbon bonding provided composites with a high sensing response performance and excellent chemical stability [94]. Fig. 12(c and f) show that the responses of ZZCl and ZZBr decreased by 6.8 % and 7.3 %, respectively, after 40 d, indicating the excellent long-term stability of these two materials.

Table 4 lists the studies of halogen quantum-dot-doped ZIF-8 gas sensors with other gas sensors, and the comparative analysis highlights the great practical potential of the sensors presented in this study for gas detection.

The experimental approach utilized the photodeposition of Pt and PbO2 to elucidate the charge-carrier transfer pathways during the transfer of CQDs. Fig. 13 (a and d) show AC-TEM images of Pt and PbO2 deposited core–shell rod-like ZZCl, respectively The AC-TEM image in Fig. 13(b) reveals that the dark Pt nanoparticles (>3 nm) are predominantly distributed near the ZnO rods within the ZIF-8 shell. Moreover, lattice fringes with a spacing of 2.27 Å, as depicted in Fig. 13(c), are exclusively deposited on the Cl-CQDs. This phenomenon suggests that, under light irradiation, the inner ZnO layer provides electrons to the Cl-CQDs, and the precursor H2PtCl6 gains electrons from the Cl-CQDs and is subsequently reduced to Pt [95]. Fig. 13(e and f) display that PbO2 is mainly distributed at the edges of the ZIF-8 shell, with lattice fringes of 2.78 Å observed on PbO2 nanoparticles exclusively deposited on ZIF-8. This observation indicates that under light irradiation, the holes were predominantly retained in the ZIF-8 shell, and the precursor Pb(NO3)2 lost electrons and underwent oxidation to form Pb. Notably, the Cl-CQDs exhibit dual functionality as electron donors and acceptors. By coordinating the transfer of charge carriers within the Zn O and ZIF-8 shells, the Cl-CQDs significantly accelerated the reaction rate [96].

The modification of the six-membered ring structure of ZIF-8 using halogen-doped CQDs was investigated in detail. The crystallographic information and atomic positions obtained from the experiments were inputted into Vesta to obtain the structure of ZIF-8 [97]. As shown in Fig. 14 (a), the standard pore size of ZIF-8 is 3.34 Å, while in Fig. 14(b), the Rietveld refinement results, the average pore size of ZZ is 3.5 Å, which indicates that the error between the calculated and experimental values is within a reasonable range. For ZZC shown in Fig. 14(c), the pore size were 3.78 Å. It is evident from the XPS data that doping with CQDs during the growth of ZIF-8 nanomaterials can alter the pore size of ZIF-8, validating the assumptions made regarding the structural changes in ZIF-8. After halogen doping, in Fig. 14(d), the pore size of ZIF-8 in ZZF is reduced to 2.86 Å, while in Fig. 14(e) for ZZCl, the pore size increases to 3.10 Å. Continuing with ZZBr, as shown in Fig. 14(f), the pore size further increases to 3.76 Å. F, Cl, and Br, which possess different electronegativities, interact differently with the metal cations. Previous studies have demonstrated that various ZIF materials exhibit specific pore sizes that are conducive to the selective sieving of gases. The flexible nature of the functional groups in ZIFs allows deformation within a certain range, facilitating the passage of molecules with diameters that are not significantly different from the pore size range [98]. We anticipate leveraging this flexibility to finely tune the structures of materials such as MOFs and COFs. The exceptional characteristics of ZIF-8, including its structural stability and hydrophobicity compared to other ZIF materials, provide researchers with a pathway to precisely regulate the structures of materials by fine doping with halogen elements and carbon quantum dots.

We employed the sorption module in Materials Studio to simulate the adsorption of gas molecules on the material using calculations based on molecular dynamics. The density distribution of the adsorbed NH3 molecules is shown in Fig. 14, revealing that the NH3 molecules are predominantly distributed within the zeolite-like structure of ZIF-8. This suggests that gas molecules can traverse the hexameric rings of ZIF-8 and interact with the internal ions, aligning with the viewpoint in the literature that the hexagonal lattice center of ZIF-8 is a strong gas adsorption site [17]. In Fig. 15 (a), the adsorption energy curve for ZZCl on NH3 indicated that the predominant contributions to NH3 adsorption by ZZCl were electrostatic forces, followed by van der Waals forces. The energy curve in Fig. 15(b) exhibits multiple peaks, indicating the presence of multiple adsorption sites. Considering the highly symmetric nature of the ZIF-8 framework, we subsequently investigated adsorption not confined to specific sites but primarily focused on the adsorption of gas molecules at a certain distance from the hexameric ring clusters. Fig. 15(c) shows that the adsorption of gas molecules requires penetration through ZIF-8. Table 5 lists the adsorption amounts and heats of different gas molecules on the material. ZZBr exhibited the highest adsorption amount and heat for CH2O compared to the other materials. Due to its larger pore size, CH2O (4.5 Å) can more easily penetrate the hexameric rings of ZIF-8 to undergo reactions. Furthermore, ZZCl demonstrated higher adsorption amounts and heat for NH3 than ZZC, resulting in a higher response to NH3. ZZF exhibited lower adsorption amounts and heat for methanol than ZZCl and ZZBr. The orange distribution in Fig. 14 also indicates that the loading of gas molecules within the ZIF-8 framework of ZZF was smaller than those of ZZCl and ZZBr. This is attributed to the smaller hexameric ring pore size of ZZF compared to the molecular diameter of methanol (3.8 Å). Consequently, we inferred that the specific reaction of ZZF with alcohols was influenced by fluorine in ZZF.

The adsorption of gas molecules onto the ZIF-8 clusters was optimized using Density Functional Theory (DFT). Fig. 16 (a, d), (b, e), and (c, f) present snapshots of the optimized geometric shapes for ZZF adsorbing C2H6O, ZZCl adsorbing NH3, and ZZBr adsorbing CH2O, respectively. Table S3 lists the energies of the system after optimization of each adsorption site. Here, the central atoms were optimized to study the influence of halogen doping on the adsorption energy. The adsorption energies, Eads of the gas molecules on the ZIF-8 clusters were calculated using the following equation:

where Etot , Egas , and EZIF denote the overall energy of the adsorbed gas molecule structure, the energy associated with the adsorbed gas, and the energy related to the ZIF-8 cluster, respectively. Comparing the adsorption energies (Table 6 ), ZZBr exhibits the strongest adsorption of CH2O among the three gases, facilitated by its larger pore size, providing a pathway for gas molecules to enter and react. ZZCl has a lower adsorption of NH3 than CH2O, and the inhibitory effect of the Cl-CQDs on the ZZCl pore size impedes larger-diameter CH2O molecules from reacting. ZZF showed significantly lower adsorption of C2H6O than NH3 and CH2O [99]. The electronegativity of F facilitates electrophilic substitution reactions with –OH in methanol and ethanol, promoting charge carrier flow and enhancing the electrochemical performance. In addition the closest distances of the gas molecules to the atoms in the frames and in the ZIFs are all greater than 3.2 Å, which suggests that there is mainly no charge transfer or complexation between the gas molecules and the frames of the ZIFs, and that the adsorption of gases on the ZIFs is mainly governed by long-range nonbonding interactions, in line with the results of the previous molecular dynamics adsorption density maps [100,101]. And the distance between halogen and gas molecules is less than 3.2 Å, suggesting that halogen reacts with gas molecules to promote the gas-sensitive response process. The HOMO-LUMO isosurface map in Fig. S5 illustrates that the LUMO densities in the electron-deficient region are mainly distributed on the Zn atoms of the transition metals in the ZIF-8 cluster model. It is hypothesized that the halogen atoms tend to interact with the Zn atoms and thus affect the bond length of the six-membered rings.

This material formed an electron-depletion layer and an effective barrier to both air and specific gases. When released back into the conduction band of ZnO, the captured electrons reduce the effective barrier and depletion layer of the electrons. Halogen doping plays a synergistic role in gas detection. In terms of charge-carrier transport, the inner ZnO, as a narrow-bandgap semiconductor, is more prone to exciting electrons to the conduction band, serving as an electron donor. Combining the positions of the ZnO and ZIF-8 conduction band and valence band energy levels in the literature [59]. along with the positions of electrons and holes during photodeposition, Fig. 17 presents the potential mechanism of an intrinsic electric field promoting charge-carrier flow. Similar to a type-II heterojunction, the ZIF-8 conduction-band electrons are transferred to the ZnO conduction band, facilitating the formation of oxygen anions. The CQDs acted as bridges, synergistically promoting electron transfer. The holes diffused from ZnO to ZIF-8.

The purification of toxic gas molecules by plant cells is the result of sub-functional work but synergistic action, which is consistent with the performance of good gas sensors. Inspired by this, we utilized halogens and CQDs to synergistically modify ZIF-8 during its formation. The synthesized materials (ZZF for alcohols, ZZCl for NH3, and ZZBr for CH2O) have specific gas response characteristics, which not only provide excellent selectivity and long-term stability, but also lower detection concentration and faster response/recovery than other sensors of the same type, as well as the composites are resistant to moisture interference due to the unique hydrophobicity of ZIF-8. CQDs not only act as a carrier for halogen doping, but also optimize the carrier transport paths of the composites as well as increase the specific surface area of the composites, which enhances the gas-sensitive properties of the composites. The weak electronegativity difference of the same main group halogen is exploited to more finely modulate the chemical bonding of the ZIF-8 imidazole ring, which affects the size of the pore size of the screened gas. In this study, a new strategy for modifying ZIF materials is proposed, and the modified materials have excellent potential for application in the field of gas sensors.
Associated content.

Schematic diagram of the sensor sensing test; rietveld refined X-ray diffraction line profile; XPS spectra of Cl element in ZZCl; XPS spectra of Zn element in ZZC, ZZF, ZZCl, ZZBr; comparison of XPS data offsets for ZZC, ZZF, ZZCl, and ZZBr; sensing curves of the ZZBr towards 500 ppm CH2O and NH3; most stable energies obtained by structural optimization of the elements F, Cl, and Br at different positions in the ZIF-8 cluster; computationally predicted HOMO, LUMO iso-surfaces of ZZF, ZZCl and ZZBr; CQDs, F-CQDs, Cl-CQDs and Br-CQDs dispersions.
